Most colonial boundary treaties tried to be explicit enough, but inconsistencies happened, and later surveyors had to interpret which system was intended.
If straight borders are easier to define, why didn’t Europe adopt more of them later?

Europe did adopt geometric borders in some contexts, but several factors limited them: - Pre-existing political geography: By the time precise surveying and modern cartography were widespread, Europe already had entrenched states and borders with historical legitimacy. Redrawing them as neat coordinate lines would have been politically explosive. - Dense populations and property rights: Straight-line borders would cut through towns, farms, and infrastructure, creating immense administrative and legal complications. - Strategic considerations: In Europe’s military history, controlling ridgelines, river crossings, and fortresses mattered. Borders often reflect those priorities. - Norms and legitimacy: European political legitimacy often rested on historical claims, cultural regions, and past treaties—less on abstract geometry. That said, you can find geometric segments in Europe: parts of the Greece–Turkey border are river-based but also treaty-defined; some borders in Eastern Europe were drawn with lines on maps during and after the World Wars, though not typically lat/long.
How did colonial powers decide where to draw lines when they had no idea about ethnic groups?

They often had partial, biased, or oversimplified information. Decisions were driven by: - Rivalry management: The primary goal in many negotiations was to prevent war between European powers by partitioning spheres of influence. - Access to resources and routes: Control of river basins, caravan routes, ports, mineral regions (sometimes rumored rather than known), and later rail corridors. - Existing coastal footholds: Boundaries often extended inland from coastal colonies. - Missionary and trading company influence: Chartered companies and missions lobbied for favorable borders. - “Ethnographic” maps: Europeans sometimes used ethnographic sketches, but these were crude and treated identities as fixed territories, ignoring intermixing, multilingual zones, and seasonal movement. Even when they tried to respect “tribal boundaries,” the premise could be flawed because many societies had overlapping authorities, tributary relationships, and flexible frontiers rather than hard lines.
Were African political boundaries before colonization less fixed?

Often, yes—though it varied widely. Many precolonial polities had frontiers that were zones of influence rather than sharply surveyed lines. Authority could fade with distance from a capital or center, and control could be seasonal, negotiated, or contested. But it’s important not to romanticize it as borderless. Some states had well-defined boundaries in practice, especially where natural barriers existed or where competing states met. There were also fortified frontiers, border towns, tributary arrangements, and recognized limits. The key difference is that European international law increasingly demanded linear boundaries with precise descriptions, whereas many African political systems operated with more flexible spatial concepts.
So when independence came, why didn’t countries renegotiate to match ethnic regions better?

Because the costs and risks were enormous: - Ethnic geography is not neatly partitioned. Many groups are intermingled; cities are diverse; identities overlap; and boundaries based on ethnicity can create new minorities and grievances. - Power politics. Any renegotiation would create winners and losers; it could invite external intervention and internal secessionist movements. - Administrative continuity. New states inherited colonial administrative structures, and changing borders would disrupt governance, taxation, and security. - OAU stance. The 1964 OAU principle of respecting inherited borders was a collective security strategy to avoid continent-wide border wars. Some adjustments did happen through bilateral agreements, but wholesale redrawing was rare. In a few cases, secession or partition occurred (e.g., Eritrea from Ethiopia; South Sudan from Sudan), but these were exceptional and often conflict-ridden.
Can we quantify how “straight” African borders are compared to Europe?

You can quantify it in a few ways, though the results depend on definitions: - Fraction of border length that is a geodesic segment or follows a parallel/meridian. - Border sinuosity: ratio of actual border length to the length of the shortest path between endpoints. Higher sinuosity indicates more winding borders (often river-following or historically jagged). - Angularity metrics: count of vertices/turning points per unit length in a simplified polyline representation. - Alignment with graticule: measure how much border segments align with N–S/E–W directions (suggesting meridian/parallel influence). Studies using GIS often find that African borders have a higher proportion of straight segments than Europe’s, but not overwhelmingly so when you consider that Africa has many long desert borders. Europe’s borders are shorter on average and more fragmented, and many are influenced by historical parcels and natural features, raising sinuosity.
Are there particular regions in Africa where borders are especially geometric?

The Sahara and Sahel stand out: Algeria–Mali, Algeria–Niger, Libya–Chad, Egypt–Sudan, parts of Mauritania–Mali, Niger–Chad. Also some southern African borders, like Botswana–Namibia (though there are river segments too), and the straight-ish stretches involving Angola/Namibia/Botswana/Zambia where colonial-era treaties intersected. In contrast, the Great Lakes region has many water boundaries, and West Africa has a mix: some straight segments, some river-based, and many borders shaped by colonial administrative units and later adjustments.
What about the Gambia? That border looks like it follows a river corridor.

The Gambia is a good counterexample to the “straight lines” narrative. Its shape reflects British control of the Gambia River corridor while France controlled the surrounding Senegal region. The boundary essentially encloses navigable access along the river from the Atlantic inland. It wasn’t purely “natural” (it’s not just the river itself), but it’s a classic case of a colonial riverine enclave/strip shaped by trade and navigation rights. The width of the corridor reflects negotiated limits of influence and practical control rather than a simple geometric line.
And Namibia’s Caprivi Strip—why does that exist?

The Caprivi Strip (now Namibia’s Zambezi Region) resulted from late-19th-century German–British negotiations. Germany wanted access to the Zambezi River, imagining it could provide a route to East Africa (this was strategically misguided because the river is not easily navigable through the Victoria Falls and rapids). Britain traded that corridor in exchange for other concessions (often summarized in relation to Heligoland–Zanzibar arrangements, though the details involve multiple agreements). The corridor’s odd shape illustrates how borders were sometimes drawn to secure “access” to a feature (a river) rather than to align with local settlement patterns. It also shows how map-based strategic thinking could misjudge physical geography.
So did Europeans think rivers were like highways everywhere?

Often, yes—especially before detailed reconnaissance. In Europe and parts of North America, rivers were major transport arteries. Colonial planners sometimes assumed similar navigability in Africa, underestimating cataracts, seasonal variability, and disease environments. The Congo is navigable in long stretches but interrupted by rapids; the Zambezi has major obstacles; many rivers are seasonal or have challenging gradients. This mismatch between map imagination and on-the-ground reality contributed to some “corridor” decisions and to the emphasis on controlling river mouths and navigable segments.
How did the concept of “watershed boundaries” come into play? Those can be natural too.

Watershed (drainage divide) boundaries—following the crest line separating river basins—were attractive because they can be stable and defensible. In mountainous terrain, divides are often clear. Colonial treaties sometimes used watershed language: “the boundary follows the watershed between the X and Y basins.” But watersheds can be ambiguous in flat terrain, and identifying the exact divide requires detailed topographic surveys. In some African regions, especially highlands (East Africa), watershed-based boundaries were used more than in deserts. In Europe, watershed boundaries are common in mountain ranges (Alps, Carpathians, Scandinavian divides).
Are there cases where a boundary was intended to follow a watershed but ended up as a straight line due to lack of data?

Yes, that pattern occurs. Treaty negotiators might agree in principle to a divide, but if maps lacked sufficient contour information, they might approximate it with a straight line between known points. Later, when better surveys revealed the true divide, disputes could arise: should the boundary follow the intended watershed (as discovered later) or the originally demarcated straight approximation? Boundary commissions sometimes resolved this by fixing the line as demarcated to avoid reopening the whole treaty, unless both parties agreed to adjust. International courts tend to weigh the treaty text, subsequent practice, and the stability principle.
Speaking of courts, how do modern international courts handle colonial border disputes?

They typically start with the relevant colonial-era legal instruments: treaties, administrative decrees, maps, and demarcation records. Key principles include: - Uti possidetis juris: inherited administrative boundaries are presumptively the international boundary. - Effectivités: evidence of actual administration and control can clarify ambiguous legal title, but usually cannot override a clear treaty title. - Stability and certainty: courts prefer interpretations that minimize disruption and align with long-standing practice. The International Court of Justice (ICJ) has handled several African boundary cases (e.g., Burkina Faso/Mali; Cameroon/Nigeria over the Bakassi Peninsula; Benin/Niger). These cases often turn on interpreting colonial documents and the weight of maps.
Maps as evidence seems tricky. How do courts treat maps?

Courts treat maps cautiously. A map is usually not a legal instrument by itself unless it’s annexed to a treaty and explicitly given legal force. Otherwise, maps can be “extrinsic evidence” that helps interpret ambiguous text or shows how parties understood a boundary. Courts look at: - Who made the map and for what purpose. - Whether both parties accepted it. - Consistency with other evidence. - Scale and accuracy. In many disputes, colonial maps conflict with one another. Courts then rely more heavily on treaty language and demarcation records.
Is the “straightness” more about deserts because it’s easier to draw and no one lives there?

Deserts amplify the effect, yes. Several reasons: - Sparse settlement (though not uninhabited): fewer towns to “anchor” a boundary, fewer administrative divisions, and less immediate resistance from dense populations. - Fewer prominent physical features: fewer perennial rivers and obvious ridge lines. - Strategic prioritization: colonial powers sometimes cared less about precise local governance in remote deserts than about preventing rival encroachment, so a coordinate line was “good enough.” - Visual contrast: in the Sahara, a long straight line stands out on a map. But “no one lives there” is misleading. Nomadic and semi-nomadic groups used these regions extensively. Straight borders could disrupt transhumance routes and customary land use, even if there were fewer permanent settlements.
What’s the relationship between straight borders and internal conflict? Is there a proven link?

The relationship is debated. It’s plausible that arbitrary borders—straight or not—can contribute to conflict by splitting communities or forcing diverse groups into one state. But conflict has many drivers: governance, resource distribution, external intervention, economic shocks, and more. Empirical studies sometimes find correlations between “artificial” borders and certain conflict patterns, but measuring “artificiality” is hard. A border could be curvy and still cut across communities; a straight border might coincide with a real cultural frontier in some places. Also, many conflicts are internal (civil wars) not strictly border disputes. So: borders matter, but “straightness” alone isn’t a reliable predictor.
Europe also has examples of arbitrary borders. Like in the Middle East, you see straight lines too. Is that the same phenomenon?

Similar, yes. In the Middle East, many borders were drawn in the context of imperial collapse (Ottoman Empire) and European mandates, with major influence from Britain and France. Straight segments appear in Syria–Iraq–Jordan–Saudi Arabia, for example, reflecting negotiated spheres and administrative convenience. The broader pattern is: when borders are created rapidly by external powers using small-scale maps and diplomatic bargaining, you tend to get more geometric lines. Africa and the Middle East share aspects of that history, though each region has its own complexities.
Back to Europe: did medieval borders follow natural features, or is that a modern story?

Medieval political geography was often fuzzy. Feudal holdings, ecclesiastical jurisdictions, and city leagues created overlapping authorities. Boundaries could be defined by who owed allegiance or paid taxes, not by a surveyed line. Natural features mattered (rivers, forests, mountain passes), but the idea of a continuous linear boundary around a state is more modern, tied to the rise of centralized states, cartography, and modern sovereignty. Over time, as states consolidated, they increasingly defined borders in linear terms and often used natural features because they were recognizable and defensible. So the “Europe always had natural borders” story is simplified. Many modern European borders are the result of 19th–20th century treaties that used both natural features and political compromises.
Can you walk through one specific African border’s creation in detail?

Take the Cameroon–Nigeria boundary, which later became famous in the Bakassi dispute. - Colonial context: Germany colonized Kamerun; Britain controlled Nigeria. They negotiated boundaries through a series of agreements (notably 1913 Anglo-German agreements) defining spheres and administrative lines. - Mixed definition: The boundary used rivers in places and straight/administrative lines in others. The Bakassi Peninsula area involved riverine and coastal geography where channels and estuaries complicate “natural” definitions. - Post-colonial inheritance: After World War I, Germany lost Kamerun, which was divided into British and French mandates. Later, parts of British Cameroons joined Nigeria or Cameroon via plebiscite. - Dispute and ICJ: Nigeria and Cameroon disputed Bakassi. The ICJ in 2002 largely favored Cameroon based on colonial treaty title (1913 agreements), despite Nigeria’s arguments about effectivités (administration and population ties). Implementation required a mixed commission and phased withdrawal. This example shows: (1) colonial documents matter; (2) “natural” coastal/river boundaries can be legally complex; (3) independence didn’t reset the lines.
Interesting. Another one: Morocco–Algeria has disputes. Is that linked to straight lines too?

The Morocco–Algeria border dispute (including the 1963 “Sand War”) is linked to colonial-era boundary ambiguity in the Sahara. France administered Algeria as part of France (not just a colony) and later reconfigured Saharan territories administratively, while Morocco had historical claims based on precolonial influence zones. The border includes desert segments where colonial definitions and administrative changes were contested. It’s less about a single straight line and more about how France’s shifting internal administrative boundaries and Morocco’s historical claims interacted with uti possidetis at independence. The resulting dispute shows how “administrative lines” can become international borders even when their legal pedigree is contested.
Are straight borders more common in former French colonies or British ones?

There isn’t a simple one-to-one. Both French and British colonial administrations used geometric lines when convenient. French West Africa has many straight segments, but so do British-influenced borders in deserts and savannas. The pattern correlates more with terrain (desert) and the timing/manner of negotiation than with one empire’s style. That said, French colonial administration was often more centralized, and French cartographic traditions were strong; British indirect rule relied on existing structures in some areas. But in boundary-making between empires, the bilateral treaty style—often using coordinates—was shared.
How did they choose which latitude or longitude? Like why the 22nd parallel for Egypt–Sudan?

Choices often reflected negotiation convenience and existing administrative reach. For Egypt–Sudan, the 22nd parallel had historical administrative significance and offered a clear, simple line separating zones of control under Anglo-Egyptian arrangements. In other cases, a particular parallel/meridian might be selected because: - It intersected a known point (river confluence, oasis). - It approximated a sphere-of-influence boundary agreed earlier. - It created a buffer between rival claims. - It was easy to describe and later survey. Sometimes the number looks arbitrary because it is: a compromise coordinate that both parties could accept without detailed local knowledge.
But longitude lines converge toward the poles. Wouldn’t that distort territory sizes?

Meridians converge, yes, so a border defined by a meridian will be closer to another meridian at higher latitudes. In Africa, most borders are in low to mid latitudes, so convergence is less dramatic than near polar regions. Still, it matters for area calculations and for how a “straight” line appears on different projections. In practice, colonial negotiators cared more about relative claims and strategic points than about equal-area fairness. Also, many borders are combinations: meridian segments plus parallels plus feature-following stretches.
How did boundary-making relate to the idea of “spheres of influence”?

“Spheres of influence” were often vague, defined as areas where a power claimed priority in making treaties with local rulers or in future annexation. They were a diplomatic device to avoid direct conflict among Europeans before effective occupation. Over time, spheres were hardened into boundaries as colonial administration expanded. The transition from sphere to border often involved: - Treaties between European powers converting vague zones into lines. - Expeditions to establish posts and “effective occupation.” - Demarcation commissions to mark the line. Because spheres were negotiated on small-scale maps, straight lines were a convenient way to convert them into formal borders.
Were local African leaders involved at all in drawing these borders?

Generally, local leaders were not meaningfully included in inter-European boundary negotiations. They were sometimes involved indirectly: - Through treaties or “protectorate” agreements that Europeans used to justify claims. - As guides or intermediaries for boundary commissions. - In some cases, as parties to local boundary arrangements between colonial administrations and local authorities. But the major international borders were primarily products of European diplomacy. Local input was limited and filtered through colonial interests.
Did any African states negotiate borders among themselves before colonization that resemble modern boundaries?

There are cases where precolonial political boundaries influenced colonial and modern ones, especially where strong states existed and Europeans incorporated existing divisions. For example, some boundaries in parts of West Africa and the Horn reflect older polities’ spheres. But “resemble modern boundaries” is tricky because modern borders are linear and exact, whereas precolonial boundaries were often zones. However, colonial administrators sometimes used existing political divisions to organize districts, which later became administrative boundaries and then international borders. So influence can be indirect rather than a direct carryover of a line.
What about Ethiopia and Liberia, which weren’t colonized in the same way—do their borders look different?

Ethiopia and Liberia are instructive. - Ethiopia maintained sovereignty (with the exception of Italian occupation 1936–1941) and expanded/contracted through wars and treaties. Its borders were shaped by negotiations with colonial powers (Italy in Eritrea and Somaliland; Britain in Sudan/Kenya; France in Djibouti) and by internal imperial expansion. The resulting borders are a mix: some follow rivers and watersheds; some are treaty lines. - Liberia, founded with support from the American Colonization Society, negotiated boundaries with Britain and France in the region. Its borders include river segments and negotiated lines; not purely geometric. So they’re not “immune” from the same international boundary-making logic; they still interacted with colonial neighbors and European treaty practices.
How do modern mapping agencies represent disputed borders? Like dotted lines?

Yes—cartographic conventions vary, but common practices include: - Dashed or dotted lines for disputed boundaries. - Different colors or patterns for claimed vs administered areas. - Notes like “boundary representation is not necessarily authoritative.” International organizations (UN) often use neutral representations or disclaimers. Commercial maps may choose one depiction based on the publisher’s country, legal risk, or data sources. Disputed borders are as much political as cartographic.
Are straight borders easier to police and manage today?

Not necessarily. Ease of management depends on terrain, infrastructure, and cross-border relationships. Straight borders in deserts can be hard to patrol because they’re long, remote, and lack natural chokepoints. A river border can be easier to monitor at bridges and ferries, but porous elsewhere. Straight borders can simplify legal description and reduce ambiguity, but they don’t automatically reduce smuggling, insurgency movement, or migration. Modern border management relies more on roads, sensors, posts, and bilateral cooperation than on the geometry of the line.
You mentioned “chokepoints.” Natural borders create chokepoints like passes, right?

Often, yes. Mountains create passes; rivers create bridges and fords. Those constrain movement and can make border control more concentrated. That’s why historically, mountain ridges and rivers were attractive defensive borders. In open desert or savanna, movement can be diffuse. Even if the border is straight, there may be no natural chokepoint. Control then depends on surveillance reach and political arrangements with local communities.
Are there examples where a straight border was later adjusted to follow a natural feature?

Yes, though not extremely common at the international level because of the stability preference. Adjustments happen when: - A straight border creates practical problems (e.g., access to water, villages split). - Both states agree to a land swap or local adjustment. - Demarcation reveals that the intended line was misplotted. One often-cited type is river boundaries being fixed to a particular channel to avoid future movement, or small rectifications around settlements. Many of these are minor and not widely known.
You said rivers can be ambulatory or fixed. What determines that?

International law distinguishes between: - Accretion (gradual, natural change): typically the boundary moves with the river if the boundary is defined as the thalweg/midline. - Avulsion (sudden change, like a flood cutting a new channel): typically the boundary stays in the old channel. But states can agree otherwise by treaty. Some treaties fix the boundary to a surveyed line regardless of river movement, especially when stability is prioritized. River commissions sometimes manage navigation channels and thus influence thalweg location, which can indirectly affect the boundary if it follows the thalweg.
Does Africa have many river commissions like Europe?

There are river basin organizations (Nile Basin Initiative, Niger Basin Authority, Senegal River Development Organization/OMVS, etc.) focused on water management, not necessarily boundary maintenance. Europe has some long-standing navigation-focused river commissions (e.g., Rhine, Danube) with deep legal histories. In Africa, transboundary water governance exists and is growing, but capacity, funding, and political coordination vary. Where river borders exist, states may have bilateral mechanisms, but they’re not always as institutionalized as Europe’s older regimes.
Let’s talk about the Sahara again. If borders there are straight, do they matter much?

They matter a lot for sovereignty, resource rights, and security—even if population density is low. Desert regions can contain: - Minerals, oil, gas (e.g., Algeria, Libya, Chad, Niger). - Trade and migration routes. - Strategic depth and military considerations. - Pastoral and nomadic livelihoods. A straight line may seem abstract, but it determines which state has jurisdiction, can grant concessions, and can deploy forces. In recent decades, Sahel security challenges have made “empty” spaces geopolitically central.
How do pipelines and resource concessions interact with borders?

Borders determine: - Which state can license extraction. - Taxation and royalties. - Environmental regulation jurisdiction. - Security responsibilities. If a border is disputed, companies may hesitate to invest due to legal risk. Conversely, resources near borders can intensify disputes. Colonial-era borders sometimes cut across geological basins; that’s common globally. Modern practice often involves unitization agreements for cross-border oil/gas fields, but that requires political cooperation.
Is there any sense in which straight borders were intended to be temporary?

Sometimes spheres-of-influence lines were provisional, pending “effective occupation” and later detailed delimitation. But many straight-line boundaries became permanent once treaties were signed and demarcation occurred. Even if negotiators saw them as practical placeholders, international law tends to treat treaty boundaries as durable unless renegotiated. So “temporary” intent is hard to prove and rarely changes outcomes unless documents explicitly state provisionality.
How did railways affect border drawing?

Railways influenced borders in several ways: - Colonial powers wanted routes from interior resource areas to ports; borders could be negotiated to secure a corridor. - Competing rail projects could motivate territorial claims to control hinterlands. - Administrative boundaries sometimes followed railway lines for logistical reasons, later becoming political boundaries. But railways were often built after initial borders were agreed; then the infrastructure had to adapt to the political map rather than the other way around. Still, in some cases, planned rail corridors shaped negotiations.
Are there any famous “straight line” borders outside Africa that help illustrate the general phenomenon?

Yes: - U.S.–Canada (49th parallel; also the Alaska–Canada boundary is complex). - Many Middle East borders (Iraq–Jordan–Saudi segments). - Australia’s state borders (many are meridians/parallels). - Parts of South America (some straight segments, though many follow rivers and mountains like the Andes). These show that geometric borders are a common outcome when large territories are partitioned with limited settlement and when negotiators prefer simple, surveyable definitions.
Australia’s state borders are straight because it’s internal administration, right? Not international war.

Exactly. Australia’s internal borders were administrative decisions within a colonial framework, drawn for governance convenience across vast sparsely settled areas. That’s analogous to colonial administrative borders in Africa that later became international borders at independence—except Australia’s remained internal within one state, so the political stakes differed. It highlights that straight lines are often a bureaucratic artifact, not a uniquely African issue.
Some people argue that straight borders prove colonialism was uniquely careless in Africa. Is that fair?

Colonial border-making in Africa was often careless with respect to local social realities, but “straightness” alone isn’t proof of unique carelessness. Similar geometric partitioning happened elsewhere under imperial or administrative expansion. What is distinctive in Africa is the combination of: - Rapid partition among multiple rival European powers, - Limited inclusion of local political systems, - The later conversion of colonial administrative lines into sovereign borders across an entire continent. So the critique is valid in substance—many borders disrupted societies—but the visual argument (“straight lines therefore uniquely careless”) is rhetorically powerful but analytically incomplete.
If you had to list the main drivers behind straight borders in Africa, what would they be?

Main drivers: 1) Diplomatic convenience in inter-imperial treaties: coordinates and straight segments are easy to describe and negotiate. 2) Limited geographic knowledge at small scales: straight lines avoid relying on uncertain river sources or watershed details. 3) Terrain: deserts and open plains lack obvious features and make straight segments visually dominant. 4) Surveying practice: lat/long-based delimitation was compatible with astronomical surveying and later demarcation. 5) Strategic bargaining: corridors, access to rivers/ports, and buffer zones were often expressed geometrically. 6) Post-independence border stability norms: inherited borders were preserved, freezing these patterns.
I want to understand the technical difference between delimitation and demarcation. Can you clarify?

Sure: - Delimitation is the legal definition of a boundary in a treaty or legal instrument—words, coordinates, and sometimes attached maps. It exists “on paper.” - Demarcation is the physical marking of that boundary on the ground—pillars, beacons, cleared lines, and detailed demarcation maps and protocols. A third term is: - Administration (or management): how the boundary is monitored, maintained, and used in practice (customs posts, patrols, cross-border agreements). Many problems arise when delimitation is vague or when demarcation was never completed.
If a border is delimited by coordinates, do you even need demarcation?

You still usually want demarcation because: - People on the ground need to know where the border is. - Enforcement and administration require clarity. - Coordinates can be misinterpreted or hard to locate without equipment. - Physical markers reduce day-to-day friction (e.g., farmers, herders, local officials). In very remote areas, states sometimes rely on “virtual” coordinate lines without dense markers, but that can increase disputes if valuable resources are discovered or if security issues emerge.
Do you think modern GIS and GPS would have prevented these straight borders?

It would have changed some details but not necessarily the overall pattern. Better data would make it easier to follow watersheds or to map communities, but political incentives matter. If the primary goal is to quickly partition territory among rival powers, negotiators may still choose simple lines even with perfect maps—because simplicity reduces bargaining complexity. However, modern tools could reduce ambiguities (exact coordinates, consistent datums) and improve demarcation accuracy. They might also make it harder to claim ignorance of social impacts, though whether that would have altered decisions is political.
How do datums affect coordinates when you convert old boundary descriptions to modern GPS?

A datum defines the reference ellipsoid and the origin/alignment used to map Earth. Old surveys might use local datums. If you take old coordinates and interpret them in WGS84 (the common GPS datum) without transformation, you can shift positions by tens to hundreds of meters, sometimes more. In boundary work, that matters. Modern commissions often: - Identify the original datum used (if documented). - Recompute coordinates in modern systems. - Prioritize physical markers placed historically (if accepted) over abstract coordinate reinterpretations. So datum transformation is a technical step with legal implications.
Do African borders have more “uncertain” segments because demarcation wasn’t completed?

In some places, yes. Several African borders were never fully demarcated during colonial times, especially in remote areas. After independence, limited resources and other priorities delayed demarcation. Some borders remain partially undemarcated or poorly marked, which can fuel local disputes, especially when resources or security issues arise. In recent decades, the African Union and regional organizations have supported border programs to demarcate and manage borders more effectively, recognizing that clarity can reduce conflict.
But wouldn’t demarcation itself cause conflict, because it forces a hard line where people used to move freely?

It can. Demarcation can formalize restrictions on movement and resource access, affecting cross-border communities. That’s why modern border management often pairs demarcation with local agreements: - Cross-border grazing arrangements, - Shared markets, - Simplified border crossing for local residents, - Joint resource management. So demarcation can reduce interstate ambiguity while still allowing social and economic permeability—if policies support it.
How do enclaves happen? Europe has them; Africa has fewer, right?

Enclaves often result from: - Historical landholdings and feudal parcels (common in Europe). - River course changes creating cutoffs. - Colonial-era administrative quirks or concessions. Africa has fewer classic enclaves because many borders were drawn at large scale with less attention to tiny parcels, and colonial powers often preferred clean lines. But Africa does have exclaves and complex points—Cabinda (Angolan exclave) is a major example, separated from Angola proper by a strip of the DRC. There are also small anomalies and tripoints with complex histories.
Cabinda—why is it separated?

Cabinda’s separation traces to Portuguese colonial claims and treaties in the late 19th century, including the Treaty of Simulambuco (1885) establishing Portuguese protectorate status over Cabinda. Later colonial boundary arrangements and the creation of the Congo Free State/Belgian Congo and French Congo shaped the corridor that cut Cabinda off from Angola proper. The result is an Angolan exclave north of the Congo River mouth, bordering both the Republic of the Congo and the DRC, with significant oil resources—making its status politically sensitive.
Tripoints seem complicated too. How are they determined?

Tripoints (where three borders meet) are determined by: - The delimitation of each pairwise boundary, - Any tri-junction agreements among the three states, - Demarcation on the ground. If two boundaries are defined but the exact meeting point is not, a tripoint can remain undefined until a trilateral commission sets it. Rivers and lakes make tripoints more complex (where exactly in a lake does the tripoint lie?). Sometimes tripoints are “approximate” until surveyed.
Do lakes create weird boundary issues? Like Lake Chad?

Yes. Lakes raise questions like: - Is the boundary a median line (equidistant from shores)? - Does it follow historical administrative lines? - How do you handle fluctuating shorelines (Lake Chad shrinks/expands seasonally and over decades)? In Lake Chad, multiple countries meet (Chad, Nigeria, Niger, Cameroon). Fluctuating water levels and shifting islands complicate local administration and can cause disputes over fishing grounds, islands, and security jurisdiction.
In Europe, do lakes have similar issues?

Yes—though often managed with clearer treaties and stable institutions. Lake Constance (Bodensee) is a famous case: Germany, Switzerland, and Austria have differing views on whether there is a defined boundary in the lake (some treat it as a condominium). Other lakes have clearly defined median lines. So lake boundaries are globally tricky; Africa isn’t unique here.

So if I summarize: Africa’s straight borders come from late, fast, externally negotiated partition plus desert terrain, while Europe’s borders are older and evolved with natural features and history. Is that accurate?

Yes, with two refinements: - Africa’s borders are not uniformly straight; many follow rivers, lakes, and other features. - Europe’s borders include many “non-natural” segments and were also reshaped rapidly in some periods (e.g., post-World War settlements), but the long-run process and dense mapping made them look more “organic.” The core contrast is the timing and mechanism: rapid diplomatic delimitation among empires versus centuries of incremental state formation.
Can you go deeper into how “effective occupation” influenced border straightness?

“Effective occupation” pushed powers to establish posts, sign treaties with local rulers, and administer territory to legitimize claims. That had two opposing effects: - It could encourage more detailed boundaries where occupation was strong and rivals were close, leading to feature-based lines or demarcation around settlements. - It also encouraged rapid claim-staking: powers wanted a legally defensible boundary quickly to lock in a sphere before a rival occupied it. In that rush, straight coordinate lines were a fast way to define limits, with the idea that details could be handled later. So effective occupation didn’t directly “cause straight lines,” but it intensified the race, which favored simple delimitations.
Did technology like telegraph lines influence borders too?

Indirectly. Telegraphs and later radio improved administrative reach and military coordination, which could expand effective control into interiors. That could: - Allow a colonial power to claim larger areas, - Make it feasible to administer remote boundaries, - Influence where strategic routes were prioritized. But telegraph lines themselves were rarely used as boundaries. More often, they were part of the infrastructure that made certain borders enforceable.
How did the idea of “natural frontiers” play out in colonial debates?

European strategic thought often idealized “natural frontiers”—rivers, mountains—as defensible and legitimate. But in colonial contexts, that idea competed with: - The need to compromise with rival powers quickly, - Limited knowledge of interior geography, - The desire to secure specific economic assets. So colonial negotiators sometimes invoked natural features when convenient (a major river as a clean divider) but used geometry when natural features were inconvenient or uncertain.
Were there cases where a natural feature was chosen, but it split a community just as badly as a straight line would?

Yes. Rivers can be central to a community’s life, with settlements on both banks, shared fishing grounds, and kinship networks. Making the river an international border can divide a community, require border controls for everyday activities, and create dual jurisdictions. Similarly, mountain ridges can split valleys that are economically integrated. So “natural” does not necessarily mean socially coherent.
Conversely, could a straight line sometimes align with social boundaries?

It can, by coincidence or because negotiators tried to approximate a known division. In open terrain, a cultural or linguistic frontier might run roughly east–west or north–south, and a parallel/meridian could approximate it. Or a straight line might separate two colonial administrative zones that already corresponded to different governance systems. But relying on coincidence is risky; many straight lines cut across complex social mosaics.
If you had to pick one border that best symbolizes “ruler-drawn,” which would it be?

Visually, the Egypt–Sudan 22nd parallel is iconic because it’s a long, clean line across desert. Another is the Algeria–Mali/Niger segments. But symbolism depends on what you want to emphasize: deserts make the geometry stark.
Are there any European borders that are surprisingly straight?

A few segments are relatively straight, though not typically as long as Saharan ones. Examples include: - Portions of the Portugal–Spain border are not straight overall, but some segments are fairly linear. - Parts of the borders in Eastern Europe drawn or adjusted in the 20th century can be relatively straight in low-relief areas. - The Finland–Russia border has long stretches that are not purely natural-feature-following and can look fairly linear at small scales. But Europe’s dense patchwork and smaller scale make long straight segments less common and less visually dominant.
How do map scales affect perception? A border might look straight at one scale but not another.

Exactly. At small scale (continent-wide map), many borders are simplified polylines; curves and minor deviations are smoothed, making borders appear straighter. At larger scale, you see kinks, local deviations, and the actual demarcation. Also, generalization algorithms in digital maps simplify lines differently depending on zoom level. That can create the impression of perfect straightness even when the real border is a sequence of slightly offset segments.
So some “straight” borders might be an artifact of map generalization?

Yes, in part. Some borders are genuinely defined as parallels/meridians, but even those are not perfectly straight on many projections, and demarcation introduces small deviations. When you zoom in, you often see: - Marker-to-marker segments, - Adjustments around settlements, - Deviations to follow practical routes. So perception is influenced by cartographic representation. Still, Africa does have a higher share of coordinate-defined segments than Europe, especially in deserts.
Another thing: why do some African borders cut right through ethnic groups while in Europe many ethnic groups got their own states?

Europe doesn’t neatly map ethnicity to states either—there are many minorities and cross-border groups (Basques, Kurds are not Europe but analogous, Hungarians in Romania/Slovakia, Russians in Baltic states, etc.). But Europe did undergo nation-state consolidation in the 19th–20th centuries, with ideologies of nationalism influencing borders and population movements (sometimes forcibly). In Africa, decolonization largely preserved colonial boundaries regardless of ethnic distribution. Also, African identities and political organization were (and are) diverse; “ethnic group equals territory equals state” is a European nationalist model that doesn’t translate cleanly. Many African states are multi-ethnic by design and necessity.
But Europe had lots of wars to make that happen.

Yes. Europe’s relatively tighter alignment between some states and national identities came at high human cost: wars, expulsions, border changes, and sometimes ethnic cleansing. So it’s not simply a “better” outcome; it’s a different historical trajectory. Africa’s choice to preserve borders aimed to avoid similar continent-wide wars, though conflicts still occurred for other reasons.
Are there any theoretical frameworks in political geography that explain straight borders?

Several relevant frameworks: - Critical geopolitics: emphasizes how power and discourse shape territorial claims; straight borders reflect imperial power and cartographic authority. - Territoriality and the modern state: linear boundaries are part of the modern sovereign state system; straightness can indicate top-down imposition and abstract space. - Political boundary theory: distinguishes antecedent boundaries (drawn before settlement), subsequent boundaries (after settlement), and superimposed boundaries (imposed on existing cultural landscapes). Many African colonial borders are superimposed; many straight ones are also antecedent in sparsely mapped areas. - Core–periphery and frontier theory: borders in frontier zones are often defined abstractly, then solidified as the state expands. These frameworks help explain why geometry appears where state power is projecting into space rather than emerging from local socio-political evolution.
Antecedent vs subsequent boundaries—can you define those with examples?

Yes: - Antecedent boundary: drawn before significant settlement or before the relevant cultural landscape is established (or before it is known/considered). Example: the 49th parallel in North America was drawn without regard to local settlement patterns at the time; many desert coordinate lines in Africa are similar. - Subsequent boundary: drawn after settlement patterns exist and often reflects them. Many European borders that follow linguistic or historical administrative lines are subsequent. - Superimposed boundary: imposed on an existing cultural landscape without regard to it. Many colonial borders in Africa are superimposed, even where settlement existed. A boundary can fit more than one category depending on perspective and time scale.
Do you think “straight borders” is the best way to talk about the issue, or is it misleading?

It’s a useful entry point because it’s visually obvious and prompts questions about history. But it can be misleading if it implies: - All African borders are straight (they aren’t), - Straightness is the main cause of problems (often it’s the mismatch between borders and social/ecological realities, which can happen with any shape), - Europe’s borders are “natural” and therefore legitimate (legitimacy comes from political processes, not from rivers). A more precise framing is: many African international boundaries were rapidly delimited by colonial powers using coordinate geometry and simplified mapping, then preserved at independence. Straightness is a symptom of that process.
If someone wanted to see this in data, what GIS workflow would you suggest?

A reasonable workflow: 1) Obtain border datasets (e.g., Natural Earth for generalized; GADM for more detailed; or official boundary shapefiles if available). 2) For each continent, compute: - Segment orientation histogram (angles of line segments) to detect N–S/E–W alignment peaks. - Sinuosity metrics for each border segment between vertices. - Proportion of border length within a tolerance of parallels/meridians (e.g., segments with bearing within ±2° of 0/90/180/270). 3) Compare distributions between Africa and Europe, controlling for terrain type: - Overlay with a land cover or aridity index to separate desert vs non-desert. 4) Visualize: - Rose diagrams of segment bearings, - Maps highlighting “graticule-aligned” segments. This would make the “straightness” claim testable and show how much is driven by deserts.
Would the dataset choice matter a lot?

Yes. Generalized datasets simplify lines and can artificially increase straightness by removing small bends. More detailed datasets show more vertices and local deviations. Also, different sources encode disputed borders differently. For a fair comparison, you’d want consistent resolution and a method that’s robust to generalization, like measuring alignment at multiple scales or using simplification to a common tolerance before computing metrics.
Is there an example where a supposedly straight border on a map is actually quite jagged due to demarcation?

Yes—many coordinate-defined borders become a “staircase” of short segments because demarcation markers were placed based on practical visibility and surveying constraints. At small scale it looks like one line; at large scale you see slight offsets. The U.S.–Canada border has some of this: it’s conceptually the 49th parallel, but demarcation introduced local irregularities and later surveying refined it. In Africa, similar effects occur in deserts where marker placement and survey error lead to small zigzags.
How do modern states handle communities split by borders?

Approaches vary: - Formal border crossings and local passes for residents. - Cross-border markets and “one-stop” border posts to facilitate trade. - Joint commissions for pastoral mobility (in some regions). - Dual citizenship allowances or special economic zones (less common). - In conflict zones, unfortunately, militarization and restrictions. The effectiveness depends on state capacity and political relations. Many African borderlands are culturally integrated despite the international line; policy can either accommodate that or exacerbate friction.
Does the African Union have an official program on borders?

Yes, the African Union Border Programme (AUBP), launched in 2007, aims to: - Support delimitation and demarcation where unclear, - Promote peaceful resolution of border disputes, - Encourage cross-border cooperation and integration. It recognizes that borders can be lines of division or zones of cooperation, and it tries to shift toward the latter while maintaining legal clarity.
Earlier you said the OAU in 1964 affirmed borders. Was that unanimous?

The principle was broadly accepted, though not without tension. Some leaders had irredentist ambitions or faced secessionist pressures. But the collective interest in preventing widespread interstate conflict led to strong support for the norm. There were exceptions and challenges—Somalia’s claims in the Horn (Greater Somalia) led to conflicts; Morocco’s claims; later Eritrea/Ethiopia issues; and others. Still, the inherited-border principle remained the dominant norm.
Somalia is interesting. Its ethnic nation spans multiple states. Did that lead to different border politics?

Yes. Somali populations live in Somalia, Ethiopia (Ogaden), Kenya (Northeastern Province), and Djibouti. The Somali Republic pursued irredentist policies in the 1960s–70s, leading to: - The Shifta War in Kenya (1963–1967), - The Ogaden War with Ethiopia (1977–1978). These conflicts illustrate the alternative path: challenging inherited borders to align with ethnic nations can trigger interstate war. That history reinforced the OAU/AU preference for border stability even when borders are imperfect.
So would you say Europe’s “natural borders” are partly the result of successful irredentism?

In some cases, yes—nationalism reshaped borders and sometimes populations to better align state and nation, though “successful” often involved coercion and tragedy. Europe’s post-World War II borders, for instance, were accompanied by massive population transfers. That produced more ethnically homogeneous states in some areas, but at enormous human cost. So the European pattern is not simply about rivers and mountains; it’s also about political projects and historical upheavals.
Let’s get back to the map aesthetics. Why do straight lines stand out so much to us?

Because humans are good at detecting geometric regularity. On a map of coastlines, rivers, and irregular terrain, a perfectly straight segment is visually salient—it signals intentional design. Curvy borders can be read as “natural” even if they’re not, because they resemble organic forms. Also, straight lines often run through areas with minimal internal features on political maps (deserts), so there’s less visual “noise,” making the line even more prominent.
If a border is a parallel, on a typical cylindrical projection it’s straight. That reinforces the impression.

Exactly. On common projections like Mercator or equirectangular, parallels and meridians are straight lines on the map. So coordinate-defined borders appear as ruler-straight on those projections. If you used a different projection, the same boundary might appear curved, but many political maps use projections that keep graticules straight, reinforcing the effect.
Are there any African borders that look curved because they follow a parallel on a different projection?

Yes—on some projections, especially conic or azimuthal projections, a parallel may appear as an arc. For example, a border along a latitude line might curve on an equal-area projection centered elsewhere. But most everyday maps of Africa use projections where lat/long lines look straight, so the border appears straight.
So the geometry is partly a cartographic convention.
Partly. The underlying legal definition may still be geometric, but the visual “ruler straight” effect is magnified by projection choice and map generalization.
Okay. What would you say to someone who insists “African borders are straight because Africans didn’t build states like Europeans did”?

That claim is historically and analytically flawed. Africa had complex state systems and political formations long before colonization—empires, kingdoms, city-states, confederations—with their own territorial logics. The straightness of many modern international borders is primarily a product of colonial-era European diplomacy and mapping practices, not an absence of African political organization. It’s more accurate to say: modern African state borders were largely imposed within the European sovereign-state system at a particular historical moment, then preserved at independence. That’s distinct from the internal development of African polities.
Are there good examples of precolonial African states with well-defined territorial control?

Yes—examples include the Ashanti Empire, the Kingdom of Kongo, the Sokoto Caliphate, the Ethiopian Empire, the Buganda Kingdom, the Zulu Kingdom, and many others. Their control mechanisms varied—tribute systems, military power, administrative provinces, control of trade routes—but they were not amorphous. Again, their “boundaries” were often not surveyed lines but zones. Still, territoriality and governance were real.
Last question for now: if you could redesign borders to be “better,” what principle would you use?

In practice, redesigning borders is extremely risky. If hypothetically optimizing, you’d likely prioritize: - Minimizing forced minority creation, - Preserving economic viability (access to ports, resources, infrastructure), - Aligning with ecological management units (watersheds), - Respecting existing local governance and mobility patterns, - Ensuring defensible and administrable boundaries. But these goals conflict, and any redrawing creates new grievances. That’s why modern policy tends to focus on making borders less harmful—through integration, cross-border cooperation, and rights protections—rather than redrawing the map.
I want to dig into one more technical thing: thalweg vs midline. Which is more common in treaties?

For navigable rivers, the thalweg principle is common: the boundary follows the deepest channel, often associated with the main navigation route. The logic is to ensure both states have access to navigation and neither can block the other by controlling the entire channel. For non-navigable rivers, treaties sometimes use the midline (median line) or one bank. Some colonial treaties were inconsistent, and later practice or court decisions clarified. The choice can be politically charged because it affects control of islands, fishing rights, and navigation.
If the thalweg shifts because the river shifts, does sovereignty over islands change?

Potentially, yes, if the boundary is ambulatory. Islands can switch sides if the main channel moves around them. That’s one reason states sometimes prefer fixed boundaries or detailed island allocations. In practice, many states manage this pragmatically, but when resources or strategic value are involved, it can become contentious.
This has been extremely helpful. Can you recommend a few classic readings or authors on boundary-making?

Key areas and authors: - International boundary law: Malcolm Shaw (international law texts), and works on boundary disputes and the ICJ. - Political geography of borders: works by David Newman, Anssi Paasi (border theory), and critical geopolitics scholars. - African border history: studies on the Scramble for Africa, and detailed historical atlases and case studies (e.g., on specific borders like Cameroon–Nigeria, Eritrea–Ethiopia). - Cartography and empire: scholarship on how mapping supported imperial governance. If you want something concrete, look for books/articles on the Berlin Conference’s aftermath, the OAU 1964 border resolution, and ICJ boundary case summaries, plus a historical atlas of Africa that reproduces treaty maps.
Thanks. I’m going to come back with more questions about specific borders later.
Any time. If you name a specific border pair or region, it’s possible to trace the exact treaties, demarcation history, and why the line looks the way it does on modern maps.
Why is Manhattan a grid but London is a maze? What determines a city's street layout?

A city’s street layout is usually the cumulative result of (1) when most of its growth happened, (2) who had the legal power to plan it, (3) the transportation technology of the era, (4) land ownership patterns, and (5) topography and hydrology. Manhattan’s grid is largely the product of a deliberate, centralized planning decision: the Commissioners’ Plan of 1811 imposed a regular street network over much of the island to simplify subdivision and real estate development. London’s “maze” is the product of incremental growth over many centuries: paths followed property lines, old field boundaries, rights-of-way, and routes to markets and river crossings. When London later expanded rapidly, it did so in many separate jurisdictions with varying degrees of planning authority, so there wasn’t one single moment where a unified grid could be imposed. Other big determinants: - Land subdivision economics: grids make parcels easy to standardize and sell. - Governance: strong municipal/state planning can impose regularity; fragmented governance tends to yield patchwork. - Era of growth: pre-industrial cities are often organic; industrial-era and colonial-era cities often have planned grids. - Transportation: streetcars and later automobiles favored wider, straighter corridors; medieval pedestrian cities didn’t need that. - Physical constraints: rivers, hills, coastlines, and floodplains often force deviations from any ideal geometry.
So is a grid basically “modern planning,” and organic patterns are “old”? Or are there modern organic cities too?

It’s correlated with age, but it’s not that simple. Grids are common in many modern planned expansions because they’re easy to survey, easy to extend, and efficient for subdividing land. But “modern planning” also produced plenty of non-grid forms: curvilinear suburban street hierarchies (loops and cul-de-sacs), radial-and-ring systems, superblocks, and even highly organic-looking layouts designed intentionally (often to calm traffic or respond to topography). Likewise, organic patterns aren’t only “old.” You can get organic patterns today when: - Topography is complex (steep terrain, coastlines, ravines). - The development model is incremental and parcel-by-parcel rather than master-planned. - A city adopts street standards that discourage through traffic (cul-de-sacs and crescents), which can look organic even though it’s planned. - Informal settlements emerge rapidly without formal surveying, later getting “regularized” in piecemeal ways. So: grids often signal a moment of strong, centralized intervention (or colonial surveying), while organic patterns often signal long-term accretion, fragmented authority, or terrain-driven constraints.
What makes a grid “good” or “bad” for how a city works? People always argue about walkability, traffic, and sunlight.

Grids have some consistent advantages and tradeoffs. Advantages: - Connectivity and route choice: many intersections mean many paths. That’s often good for walking and cycling because you can take direct routes and avoid big detours. - Legibility: addresses and navigation can be straightforward. - Resilience: if one street is blocked, alternatives exist. - Fine-grained blocks: when blocks are short, the pedestrian environment can be lively because there are frequent corners, storefront opportunities, and choices. Tradeoffs: - Traffic distribution: lots of connected streets can distribute car traffic into neighborhoods unless you manage it (one-way systems, traffic calming, modal filters). - Safety at intersections: more intersections can mean more conflict points, but design matters (narrower lanes, daylighting corners, protected crossings). - Monotony and orientation: a perfect grid can feel repetitive; without landmarks it can be disorienting in a different way. - Topography and drainage: forcing a grid onto hills can create impractically steep streets, erosion issues, or expensive grading. Sunlight and wind: grid orientation matters. In dense cities, the angle of streets relative to the sun affects how much light reaches street level; building height and street width ratios (the “canyon”) matter a lot. Wind can be channeled along long straight corridors. Organic patterns: - Often respond well to terrain and can create sheltered microclimates. - Can be charming and human-scaled, but sometimes lack connectivity (especially if later retrofitted for cars with dead-ends). The “best” layout is usually less about geometry and more about the network’s connectivity, block size, multimodal design, and land use patterns.
Manhattan’s grid is famous, but it’s not perfectly uniform, right? What are the main exceptions and why?

Correct—Manhattan’s grid is not perfectly uniform, and the exceptions are informative. Key exceptions: - Broadway: it predates the 1811 plan and follows an older diagonal alignment (partly tracing Indigenous and early colonial routes). It cuts across the grid, creating odd-shaped blocks and notable public spaces (Times Square, Herald Square, Madison Square). - The street numbering system isn’t perfectly consistent near the southern tip: Lower Manhattan developed earlier with irregular streets tied to the colonial-era shoreline, fortifications, and property lines. - Topography and existing roads: some parts of the island—especially northern Manhattan—interact with ridges and valleys, and while the grid was imposed, there are deviations and discontinuities. - Parks and institutional superblocks: Central Park interrupts the grid; so do large campuses, housing projects, rail yards, and waterfront infrastructure. - The width hierarchy: avenues are wider than cross streets, and some streets were later widened or altered. The Commissioners’ Plan specified standard widths, but implementation varied. The 1811 plan was a land-and-real-estate framework more than a detailed urban design manual. It prioritized regular lots and easy extension over scenic vistas or responding delicately to terrain.
London feels like it has multiple “centers” and weird angles. Is that because it absorbed villages?

That’s a major reason. London grew by absorbing many distinct settlements—villages, market towns, ecclesiastical precincts—each with its own internal street network oriented to local landmarks (a church, a market, a bridge, a manor). When they merged, the seams remained: roads that once connected separate places became major arteries, and the local street patterns around each center retained their orientation. Other contributors: - The River Thames and historical crossing points: bridges and fords anchored routes. - Medieval and early modern property boundaries: streets often aligned with parcels, walls, and rights-of-way. - Piecemeal redevelopment: fires, railways, and later road schemes created interventions, but rarely a citywide reset. - Governance fragmentation: many parishes, boroughs, and later authorities meant planning decisions were local. So London’s “maze” is not random; it’s a palimpsest—layers of history, with each layer leaving traces.
If grids are so efficient, why didn’t London just impose one in the 1800s when it was expanding?

It did impose regularity in many places—but not as a single unified grid across the whole metropolis. Reasons a Manhattan-style imposition was difficult: - Existing development: central London was already dense and valuable; wholesale clearance to re-grid would have been politically and financially enormous. - Property rights: assembling land at scale is hard when ownership is fragmented. Grids are easiest when a single authority controls large tracts before development. - Incremental expansion: London’s growth happened through many estates and speculative developments. Some used regular street patterns (often small grids), but each estate planned its own piece. - Different planning goals: 19th-century London interventions often focused on sanitation, rail access, and selective boulevards rather than a uniform street lattice. - Infrastructure constraints: rail lines, canals, and later arterial roads cut through patterns, creating discontinuities. In short: London’s growth was “additive” and multi-authored; Manhattan’s grid was “imposed” over largely undeveloped land (at least compared to London’s core).
How much does topography really matter? Manhattan has hills too, but it still got a grid.

Topography matters, but its influence depends on how strongly the planners insist on geometry versus adapting. Manhattan: The plan prioritized a simple surveyable framework. In many places, the grid was cut into bedrock; hills were leveled, valleys filled, and shorelines extended. That’s expensive and environmentally disruptive, but it was feasible given the value of land and the era’s willingness to reshape terrain. In contrast, cities with steep, irregular terrain often adapt streets to contours to reduce grades (easier for walking, animals, and later vehicles). A contour-following street can look “organic,” but it’s rational engineering: it minimizes slope and earthworks. Topography pushes against grids when: - Slopes exceed practical thresholds (for comfort and safety). - Drainage would create dangerous runoff along steep straight streets. - Rock outcrops, ravines, or waterways make straight alignments costly. Some gridded cities compromise by: - Rotating grids in different districts to fit terrain. - Using terraced streets, switchbacks, or stair streets. - Allowing discontinuities where slopes are extreme.
Can you give examples of cities that are strongly gridded but not “American colonial” grids?

Yes—grids appear across cultures and eras. Examples: - Barcelona (Eixample): A planned 19th-century expansion with a distinctive grid of octagonal blocks (chamfered corners) designed for light, ventilation, and turning radii. It’s a grid, but with deliberate geometric modifications. - Kyoto: A historical grid influenced by Chinese capital planning principles; it’s much older than modern Western planning. - Parts of Tokyo: Not fully gridded, but some districts have regular block patterns from land readjustment after disasters; still, Tokyo overall is a patchwork. - Chandigarh: A 20th-century planned city in India with a sector-based grid and hierarchical road network. - Melbourne (Hoddle Grid): A planned grid with wide streets and lanes, later surrounded by varied patterns. - Addis Ababa has some planned axes and grids in expansions; many African capitals combine planned grids with organic growth. The grid is a versatile tool: it can be used for land division, military control, ceremonial planning, ventilation, or administrative clarity.
Barcelona’s chamfered corners—were they mainly for traffic? I’ve heard multiple stories.

They served multiple intended and later-realized functions. In the Eixample plan, the chamfers (cut-off corners) were associated with: - Improved visibility at intersections: better sightlines reduce conflict. - More turning space: useful for carriages historically and later for vehicles. - Increased intersection space: room for gatherings, loading, and later street furniture. - Better light and air: widening intersections reduces the “canyon” effect. Over time, traffic engineering did use that space—sometimes in ways that conflict with the original social and environmental aspirations. Contemporary Barcelona has been rethinking intersection space through “superblocks” and traffic calming, which is in some sense a modern reinterpretation of that generous geometry.
You mentioned “superblocks.” Are those like anti-grids?

They can be layered on top of grids or used in non-grid contexts. A superblock is basically a large block (or group of blocks) where through traffic is limited, and interior streets prioritize local access, walking, cycling, and public space. In a grid context: - You can keep the grid’s connectivity for walking/cycling while reducing car permeability inside the superblock. - Perimeter streets handle through traffic; interior streets become low-speed or pedestrian-priority. - It’s a way to “tune” a grid: maintain fine-grain access but reduce cut-through driving. In non-grid contexts: - Superblocks can exist as large housing estates or campus-like developments, sometimes with poor connectivity if not designed carefully. So they’re not anti-grid; they’re a mobility management overlay. The underlying geometry matters less than how movement is regulated.
How do planners measure “connectivity” in a street network? Is there a standard metric?

There are several common metrics, each capturing different aspects. Basic intersection-based measures: - Intersection density: intersections per unit area (e.g., per square kilometer). Higher typically correlates with walkability. - Link-node ratio: number of street segments (“links”) divided by number of nodes (intersections and dead ends). Higher values generally mean more connectivity. - Proportion of 4-way intersections vs 3-way vs cul-de-sacs: 4-way tends to increase route choice but can raise conflict points if not designed safely. Network science measures: - Average shortest path length: how many steps/turns between random points. - Betweenness centrality: identifies streets that carry many shortest paths (potential bottlenecks). - Closeness centrality: how near a node is to all others in the network. - Circuity: ratio of network distance to straight-line distance for typical trips. Urban design measures: - Block size and block perimeter: smaller blocks increase permeability. - Effective walking catchment: how far you can reach within a time threshold along the network (not “as the crow flies”). No single metric is “the” standard; analysts pick based on the question (walking access, traffic flow, resilience, etc.).
Circuity seems important. Organic cities feel like they have high circuity. Is that always true?

Often, but not always. Organic patterns can have: - High circuity if streets meander, terminate, or avoid direct connections. - Low circuity if the network has many small passages and shortcuts (common in some historic cores), even if the geometry looks irregular. Conversely, planned suburbs can have very high circuity despite being “modern” and intentionally designed: cul-de-sacs and limited connections force long detours for short crow-fly distances. So circuity depends more on connectivity and directness than on whether streets are straight. A slightly curving but well-connected network can have low circuity; a straight but discontinuous network can have high circuity.
What about radial cities—like Paris with boulevards radiating out. Are those planned for defense or traffic?

Both, depending on the era and the specific intervention. Radial-and-ring patterns can emerge from: - Historical gates and walls: roads radiate from gates to regional destinations; later rings form where walls were demolished (ring roads, boulevards). - Ceremonial planning: emphasizing vistas and monuments. - Traffic distribution: radials funnel movement to/from the center; rings allow circumferential movement without entering the core. Paris is a layered case: - Medieval Paris had organic streets. - 19th-century interventions under Haussmann introduced wide boulevards that cut through the fabric, improving circulation, sanitation (in part), and state control. The “defense” or “crowd control” narrative exists, though it’s intertwined with modernization goals and politics. - The result is not a pure radial plan but a hybrid: fragments of old fabric, new diagonals, and later ring roads. Radial systems can be efficient for center-focused travel but can overload central nodes if jobs and services are highly centralized.
Is there a way to predict what pattern a city will develop just from its geography—like rivers and coastlines?

You can predict constraints and likely corridors, but not the exact pattern, because institutions and land policy are decisive. Geography tends to shape: - Main axes: along ridgelines, river terraces, coastlines, and valley floors where grades are easier. - Crossing points: bridges, fords, and narrow points become hubs. - Growth direction: away from floodplains, toward ports, along rail corridors, or around protected areas. - Neighborhood boundaries: rivers, steep slopes, and rail yards create edges. But whether those corridors become a grid, a radial system, or a patchwork depends on: - Land tenure: large estates vs small parcels. - Survey and cadastral systems. - Planning authority and timing. - Economic drivers (port trade, manufacturing, administrative capital, etc.). So geography sets the “cost surface,” but governance and history choose the “network design.”
Speaking of survey systems, is the grid partly about measurement technology?

Yes. Survey technology and cadastral practices strongly influence street regularity. Grids are easier when: - You have standardized units and instruments (chains, theodolites, later total stations). - Land is being subdivided in large tracts. - There’s a legal framework for straight property lines and rectilinear parcels. In periods or places where: - Boundaries are defined by natural features (“from the oak tree to the creek”) or customary rights, - Mapping is less precise or less centralized, - Land is subdivided incrementally, you tend to get irregular parcel shapes and therefore irregular streets. Even with good technology, though, politics matters: you can survey a grid, but you still need the authority to implement it over existing claims.
I’m curious about block size. Manhattan blocks are long and skinny. Why did they choose that, and how does it affect city life?

Manhattan’s typical block dimensions (especially in much of the grid) reflect a real-estate logic: maximize the number of lots with street frontage while minimizing the amount of street to build and maintain. Long blocks between avenues: - Reduce the number of cross streets, which reduces infrastructure costs. - Create more continuous frontage along avenues (valuable commercial corridors). - But they can be less permeable for pedestrians if mid-block crossings are limited. Skinny blocks: - Encourage through movement along avenues and certain cross streets. - Can concentrate retail on avenues and key cross streets, leaving mid-block areas quieter or more residential (depending on zoning and building types). Effects on city life: - Walkability is still high because there are many parallel routes, but the long blocks can make some trips feel longer than in a fine-grain grid with short blocks. - Traffic can move efficiently along avenues, but the hierarchy can also make certain corridors congested. - The regularity supports transit routing and service coverage. Block size is one of the most consequential design variables: even within a grid, different block dimensions create different urban experiences.
If you were designing a new neighborhood today, would you pick a grid?

I’d pick a connected network with small blocks and multiple route choices, but not necessarily a perfect orthogonal grid. A pragmatic approach many planners favor: - A permeable street network (lots of connections) for walking and cycling. - A limited number of higher-capacity streets for buses and essential vehicle movement. - Traffic calming and design speeds that match the intended use. - Blocks sized to support active frontages and frequent crossings (often 80–120 meters per side is a common target in walkable districts, though context matters). - Alignment that responds to topography and existing natural features, rather than forcing strict orthogonality. So: “grid-like connectivity” is often desirable; “rigid grid regardless of terrain” is not always.
How do parks fit into this? Central Park is like a giant void in the grid. Is that good planning or a disruption?

It’s both: a disruption to the street network and a major urban asset. Benefits: - Large parks provide ecological services (cooling, stormwater absorption, habitat), recreation, and mental health benefits. - They can increase adjacent property values and attract institutions and cultural uses. - They create a large, legible landmark that helps orientation. Costs/tradeoffs: - They break connectivity: fewer cross routes for vehicles and even for pedestrians in some cases (depending on park design and entrances). - They can create edge conditions: major perimeter roads can become barriers. - They can concentrate traffic on limited crossings. Whether it’s “good” depends on how the park interfaces with surrounding streets: - Frequent, safe crossings and entrances improve permeability. - Active edges (buildings facing the park) can make it safer and more lively. - Transit access and bike routes through/around can mitigate the barrier effect. Central Park is a deliberate interruption that changed Manhattan’s urban form profoundly—arguably for the better, but it required accepting network discontinuity.
London has lots of smaller parks and squares. Does that relate to its development pattern?

Yes. London’s pattern of garden squares, commons, and parks is closely tied to estate development and incremental planning. In many districts: - Landowners developed residential areas around private or semi-private garden squares, using them as amenities to raise property values. - Commons and royal parks remained as large open spaces, but many smaller green spaces were inserted as part of neighborhood-level schemes. This produces a distributed green network rather than a single central park dominating the grid. It fits an incremental city: each estate or district adds its own open spaces, leading to a patchwork of greens with varying access rules and designs.
How do street patterns affect transit? Does a grid make buses easier?

Generally, yes, grids can be transit-friendly, but with caveats. For buses: - Straight, continuous corridors simplify routing and make service legible. - Multiple parallel streets allow route options and diversions. - Frequent intersections can slow buses unless signal priority and stop spacing are managed. For rail/metro: - Station spacing and catchment areas interact with block size and permeability. - A connected street network increases the effective walk shed to stations.
